data.source: Self-report paper-and-pencil surveys administered to participants before the weekend (Time 1), at the end of the weekend (Time 2), and at the end of the following workweek (Time 3).
data.notes: Data were collected using paper-based questionnaires; potential self-report bias noted.

method.description: Participants completed questionnaires at three time points measuring weekend recovery experiences (relaxation, mastery, control, psychological detachment, non-work hassles) and discrete affective states (joviality, self-assurance, serenity, hostility, fear, sadness, fatigue). Confirmatory factor analyses validated measurement models, and hierarchical regression analyses examined how weekend experiences predicted affective states at Time 2 and Time 3 while controlling for baseline affect and demographics.
steps: Recruit 229 preschool teachers in Germany and obtain informed consent.
Administer Time 1 survey on Friday after the workweek to measure baseline affective states and demographics.
Administer Time 2 survey on Sunday evening to capture weekend recovery experiences and affective states.
Administer Time 3 survey on Friday of the following workweek to measure affective states again.
Run confirmatory factor analyses to verify distinct constructs for recovery experiences and seven affective states.

Conduct hierarchical regression analyses: Step 1 enter control variables and baseline affect; Step 2 enter weekend recovery variables (relaxation, mastery, control, detachment, hassles) to predict Time 2 and Time 3 affective states.
Interpret coefficients, p-values, and variance explained.
method.models: Hierarchical multiple regression; Confirmatory factor analysis (CFA)
method.outcome_variable: Discrete affective states (joviality, self-assurance, serenity, hostility, fear, sadness, fatigue)
method.independent_variables: Weekend recovery experiences: relaxation, mastery, control, psychological detachment; non-work hassles
method.control_variables: Baseline (Time 1) levels of each affective state; demographic variables

results.summary: Weekend recovery experiences significantly influenced employees’ affect. Relaxation and mastery improved positive affective states and reduced certain negative states. Psychological detachment enhanced serenity and other positive affects and lowered negative affects. Non-work hassles consistently increased negative affective states. Some effects (e.g., mastery) were immediate but faded by the end of the following workweek, whereas others (e.g., detachment on serenity) persisted.

metadata.original_paper_title: The Weekend Matters: Relationships between Stress Recovery and Affective Experiences
